topics.title: Familiarization of R Environment and R Studio, Installing and Using Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL308 - STATISTICAL METHODS LAB (R LANGUAGE)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of R Environment and R Studio, Installing and Using Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**R** is an open-source, free, statistical programming language and software environment specifically designed for statistical computing, data analysis, graphical representation, and scientific reporting. It was developed by **Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman** at the University of Auckland, New Zealand, and is now maintained by the **R Development Core Team**. R is the de-facto standard for data science, bioinformatics, econometrics, and statistical research worldwide.

**RStudio** (now called **Posit IDE**) is a powerful, free, open-source **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** for R. It provides a user-friendly graphical interface layered on top of the bare R console, offering a code editor, debugging tools, workspace browser, plot viewer, package manager, and help system — all bundled into a single elegant dashboard.

The **R Environment** refers to the complete ecosystem surrounding the R language, including:
- The base R installation (binaries + standard libraries)
- The **CRAN** (Comprehensive R Archive Network) repository of contributed packages
- The global user workspace (`.RData`) and command history (`.Rhistory`)
- The search path of loaded packages and attached datasets

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCBL308):** Experiment 2 specifically tests the student's ability to navigate the R/RStudio interface, install external packages from **CRAN**, load them into the active session, and verify their installation. This experiment forms the *foundation* for every subsequent statistical experiment in the lab record.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of **R** as the **engine of a car** — it is the raw machinery that performs the actual computation. You can drive a car by talking directly to the engine, but it is uncomfortable, dangerous, and inefficient.

Think of **RStudio** as the **dashboard, steering wheel, gear-shift, and air-conditioned cabin** of that car — it does *not* replace the engine, but it gives you a comfortable, organized, and safe way to operate it. You can see your speed (variables), fuel level (memory usage), engine temperature (session info), and even plug in extra accessories (install packages) on demand.

Think of **CRAN packages** as **aftermarket accessories** for your car — just as you might install a GPS unit, a better music system, or a turbocharger to add new capabilities, you install R packages to add new statistical functions, plotting libraries, or machine-learning algorithms that are not part of the base R installation.

| Component | Car Analogy | Real R Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Engine | Raw R | `R.exe` / `Rscript` |
| Dashboard | RStudio IDE | `rstudio.exe` |
| GPS Accessory | ggplot2 package | `install.packages("ggplot2")` |
| Garage | CRAN Repository | `https://cran.r-project.org` |
| Driver's Memory | Global Environment | The "Environment" pane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction to Remember for Exams:** R is the *language* and *engine*. RStudio is *one* of several IDEs for R (others include Jupyter, VS Code, Emacs ESS). You can run R *without* RStudio, but you cannot run RStudio *without* R installed first. R must always be installed **before** RStudio.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Versions

- **Latest stable R version (as of 2024):** **R 4.4.x** (codenames follow the tradition of "One Tree Planted" — e.g., *Puppy Cup*).
- **RStudio version:** **Posit IDE 2024.x**.
- **CRAN hosts:** Over **21,000+** contributed packages as of 2024.
- **Default R working directory location:**
  - **Windows:** `C:\Users\<username>\Documents`
  - **Linux/Mac:** `~/` (home directory)
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the RStudio Interface (4-Pane Layout)

When you launch RStudio for the first time, the window is divided into **four primary panes**. Knowing every pane is essential for the lab viva and KTU practical exam.

1. **Source Pane (Top-Left) — The Editor**
   - Where you *write* and *save* `.R`, `.Rmd`, and `.Rpres` files.
   - Supports syntax highlighting, auto-indent, code-folding, and `Ctrl+Enter` to run a line.

2. **Console Pane (Bottom-Left) — The Heart of R**
   - The interactive R prompt (`>`).
   - Commands typed here are executed **immediately** and **not saved** permanently.
   - This is where output, errors, and warnings appear.

3. **Environment/History Pane (Top-Right)**
   - **Environment tab:** Lists all currently loaded variables, datasets, functions, with their type and dimensions (e.g., `x: num [1:100] 1 2 3 …`).
   - **History tab:** A log of every command run in the session. Click any line to re-send it to the console.

4. **Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer Pane (Bottom-Right)**
   - **Files:** A file browser of the current working directory.
   - **Plots:** Displays all generated graphs in a scrollable gallery.
   - **Packages:** A clickable checklist of installed packages with checkmarks for loaded ones.
   - **Help:** R documentation viewer.
   - **Viewer:** For interactive HTML widgets and Shiny apps.

### 2.2 The Logic of Package Management — Step-by-Step Workflow

A **package** in R is a formally structured collection of R functions, datasets, compiled code (C/C++/Fortran), help files, and vignettes bundled together. The lifecycle of using a package in R follows a strict 3-stage protocol:

**Stage 1 — Install (Download to Hard Disk)**
The package binary is downloaded from the CRAN mirror and stored permanently in the local R library directory (e.g., `C:\Program Files\R\R-4.4.1\library` on Windows). This only needs to be done **once** per machine.

**Stage 2 — Load (Attach to Active Memory)**
Loading brings the package from the hard disk into the current R session's search path. This must be done **in every fresh R session**. After loading, all the package's functions become directly accessible by their short name (e.g., `ggplot()` instead of `ggplot2::ggplot()`).

**Stage 3 — Use (Invoke Functions)**
Call the package's functions, datasets, or help files in your script.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake Alert:** Many beginners call `install.packages("dplyr")` *every* time they open R. This is wrong. You install **once** and load **every session**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Essential R Commands

| Operation | R Command | Example / Argument | Persistence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Check R version | `R.version` or `version` | `version$version.string` | — |
| Check working directory | `getwd()` | Returns path string | Per-session |
| Set working directory | `setwd("path")` | `setwd("C:/lab/data")` | Per-session |
| List installed packages | `installed.packages()` | Returns matrix | — |
| Install package from CRAN | `install.packages("name")` | `install.packages("dplyr")` | **Once** |
| Install multiple packages | `install.packages(c("a","b"))` | `install.packages(c("ggplot2","tidyr"))` | **Once** |
| Load package | `library(name)` or `require(name)` | `library(ggplot2)` | Per-session |
| Use function without loading | `pkg::function()` | `dplyr::filter(df, x>5)` | Per-call |
| Update all packages | `update.packages()` | Prompts confirmation | — |
| Get help on function | `?func` or `help(func)` | `?mean` | — |
| List attached packages | `search()` | Returns search path | — |
| Unload a package | `detach("package:name")` | `detach("package:dplyr")` | Per-session |
| List files in directory | `list.files()` | Returns character vector | — |
| Save workspace | `save.image()` | Saves `.RData` | — |
| Load workspace | `load(".RData")` | Restores session | — |
| Clear console | `Ctrl+L` | Shortcut only | — |
| Quit R session | `q()` | Prompts save | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The R ecosystem is indispensable across modern engineering disciplines:

- **Civil Engineering:** Structural reliability analysis (`survival` package), hydrological time-series forecasting, and concrete mix-design optimization.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Six-Sigma quality control charts, vibration signal analysis, and reliability engineering (Weibull distribution fitting).
- **Electrical/Electronics:** Signal processing (`signal` package), control system design, and power-system load forecasting.
- **Computer Science:** Machine learning (`caret`, `randomForest`), natural language processing (`tm`), and network analysis (`igraph`).
- **Biomedical/Biotechnology:** Genomic data analysis (Bioconductor), clinical trial statistics, and epidemiological modeling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The R community operates on the principle of **"reproducible research"** — every analysis must be scripted (not point-and-click), so the entire workflow can be re-run, audited, and verified. This is the philosophical reason why R dominates academic and regulatory submissions (e.g., FDA clinical trials).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since Experiment 2 is procedural (R environment + package management) rather than purely mathematical, the "derivations" below are **exhaustive, line-by-line workflows** that you must memorize for the KTU lab record and viva. Every keystroke is captured.

### 3.1 Workflow A — Verifying the R Installation

**Step 1: Launch RStudio.**
The application is typically found at `Start → All Programs → RStudio` (Windows) or `/Applications/RStudio.app` (Mac).

**Step 2: Inspect the version string in the console.**
Type the following command at the `>` prompt and press **Enter**:

```r
version$version.string
```

**Step 3: Read the output.**
R responds with a quoted string of the form:
`[1] "R version 4.4.1 (2024-06-14)"`

**Step 4: Cross-verify the R home directory path.**

```r
R.home("bin")
```

This returns the absolute path of the R binary, e.g., `"C:/PROGRA~1/R/R-44~1.1/bin/x64"`.

> The command `R.home("bin")` is derived from the R internals: the R binary searches the system environment variable `R_HOME` to locate its own executable. The function `R.home(component)` is a wrapper that queries this variable and concatenates the requested sub-directory.

### 3.2 Workflow B — Installing a Package from CRAN

**Step 1: Choose a CRAN mirror (only the first time).**
When you call `install.packages()` for the very first time, R will pop up a dialog box listing dozens of CRAN mirrors (e.g., *India (Bangalore)*, *USA (CA 1)*, *Germany (Berlin)*). Select the geographically closest mirror to minimize latency.

**Step 2: Issue the install command.**

```r
install.packages("MASS")
```

**Step 3: Read the console output.**
R will:
1. Connect to the selected CRAN mirror via HTTPS.
2. Download the binary tarball `MASS_7.3-60.tgz` (or `.zip` on Windows).
3. Unpack it into your local library directory.
4. Compile any C/Fortran source (if installing from source).
5. Display a final `DONE (MASS)` confirmation.

**Step 4: Verify the installation succeeded.**

```r
installed.packages()[,1]
```

This returns a character vector of all installed package names. Locate `"MASS"` in the list.

> [!NOTE]
> The `installed.packages()` function returns a **matrix** where the first column (`[,1]`) is the **Package** name, the second is the **LibPath** (install location), and the third is the **Version** string. Indexing with `[,1]` is a standard idiom for extracting just the names.

### 3.3 Workflow C — Loading a Package into the Active Session

**Step 1: Issue the library command.**

```r
library(MASS)
```

**Step 2: Confirm the package is now in the search path.**

```r
search()
```

The output is a character vector showing the order of attached packages. You should see `"package:MASS"` in the list, for example:
```
[1] ".GlobalEnv"        "package:MASS"      "tools:rstudio"    
[4] "package:stats"     "package:graphics"  "package:methods"   
[7] "Autoloads"         "package:base"
```

**Step 3: Use a function from the package without prefix.**

```r
survey <- mammals
head(survey, 3)
```

This calls the `mammals` dataset and the `head()` function, both made available by `MASS`.

> **Search-Path Derivation:** R resolves a function name by searching *upward* through the search path in order. When you call `head()`, R first looks in the Global Environment, then in `package:MASS`, then in `package:base`, etc. The first match wins. This is why functions in *later*-loaded packages can **mask** identically named functions in earlier ones.

### 3.4 Workflow D — Using a Function Without Loading (Namespacing)

Sometimes you only need **one** function from a package and want to avoid cluttering the search path. Use the `::` operator:

```r
dplyr::filter(mtcars, cyl == 6)
```

This explicitly calls the `filter` function from the `dplyr` namespace **without** attaching the entire `dplyr` package via `library()`. This is the **best practice** in production R scripts because:
1. It documents *which* package each function comes from.
2. It avoids name-masking conflicts (e.g., `filter` exists in both `stats` and `dplyr` with different behaviors).
3. It makes scripts more portable and reproducible.

### 3.5 Workflow E — Loading Built-in Datasets

R ships with over **100** classic datasets built into the base installation. To list all available datasets:

```r
data(package = "base")$results[, "Item"]
```

To load and view a specific dataset:

```r
data("mtcars")
head(mtcars, 5)
```

Output:
```
                   mpg cyl disp  hp drat    wt  qsec vs am gear carb
Mazda RX4         21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.620 16.46  0  1    4    4
Mazda RX4 Wag     21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.870 17.02  0  1    4    4
Datsun 710        22.8   4  108  93 3.85 2.320 18.61  1  1    4    1
Hornet 4 Drive    21.4   6  258 110 3.08 3.215 19.44  1  0    3    1
Hornet Sportabout 18.7   8  360 175 3.15 3.440 17.02  0  0    3    2
```

### 3.6 Workflow F — Managing the Working Directory

```r
# Step 1: Find the current working directory
getwd()
# [1] "C:/Users/Student/Documents"

# Step 2: Set a new working directory
setwd("C:/KTU/PCCBL308/Experiment2")
getwd()
# [1] "C:/KTU/PCCBL308/Experiment2"

# Step 3: Verify the directory exists and list its files
list.files()
# [1] "lab_record.R" "data.csv"     "report.pdf"
```

> **Path-Conversion Logic (Windows-Specific):** In R strings, the backslash `\` is an **escape character**. Therefore, Windows paths must use either **forward slashes** (`"C:/KTU/data"`) or **double backslashes** (`"C:\\KTU\\data"`). Using a single backslash will throw a syntax error or silently corrupt the path.

### 3.7 Workflow G — Updating and Removing Packages

```r
# Update every installed package to its latest CRAN version
update.packages(ask = FALSE)

# Remove an installed package
remove.packages("MASS")

# Check the package version
packageVersion("MASS")
# [1] '7.3.60'

# View all functions exported by a package
ls("package:MASS")
```

### 3.8 Summary Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Correct Command | Wrong Command (and why) |
|---|---|---|
| First-time install | `install.packages("dplyr")` | `library(dplyr)` (no install) |
| Every new session | `library(dplyr)` | `install.packages("dplyr")` (wasteful) |
| Single function use | `dplyr::filter(...)` | `library(dplyr); filter(...)` (clutters) |
| Get help | `?mean` or `help("mean")` | `help(mean)` (ambiguous for some names) |
| Check installation | `installed.packages()[,1]` | `library(pkg)` (silently fails if missing) |
| Working directory | `setwd("C:/lab")` | `setwd("C:\lab")` (escape error) |
| Save everything | `save.image("mywork.RData")` | `save(x, y)` (only saves x and y) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The RStudio 4-Pane IDE Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph win["RStudio Application Window"]
        direction TB
        subgraph paneTL["SOURCE PANE - Top Left"]
            ed1["R Script Editor"]
            ed2["Rmd Notebook"]
            ed3["Ctrl plus Enter runs line"]
        end
        subgraph paneBL["CONSOLE PANE - Bottom Left"]
            co1["R Prompt greater than sign"]
            co2["Command Output"]
            co3["Errors and Warnings"]
        end
        subgraph paneTR["ENVIRONMENT PANE - Top Right"]
            en1["Variables List"]
            en2["Datasets Active"]
            en3["History Tab"]
        end
        subgraph paneBR["OUTPUT PANE - Bottom Right"]
            ou1["Files Browser"]
            ou2["Plots Gallery"]
            ou3["Packages Checklist"]
            ou4["Help Documentation"]
            ou5["Viewer for HTML"]
        end
    end
    ed1 -->|Sends code| co1
    co1 -->|Updates| en1
    en1 -->|Drives| ou2
    paneTR <--> paneBR
    paneTL <--> paneBL
    style win fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style paneTL fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600
    style paneBL fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style paneTR fill:#f5e6ff,stroke:#660066
    style paneBR fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Package Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NotInstalled: Initial State
    NotInstalled --> Downloaded: install.packages called
    Downloaded --> InstalledOnDisk: Binary unpacked to library
    InstalledOnDisk --> Loaded: library call
    Loaded --> Usable: Functions accessible
    Usable --> Loaded: Already in session
    Usable --> Detached: detach call
    Detached --> InstalledOnDisk: Still on disk
    InstalledOnDisk --> Removed: remove.packages call
    Removed --> NotInstalled
    Loaded --> [*]: Session ends
    note right of InstalledOnDisk
        Persistent across R sessions
        Stored in library directory
    end note
    note right of Loaded
        Volatile
        Resets when R quits
    end note
```

```mermaid
    p2 -->|NO| p3

    p3b --> p3c
    p3c --> p3d

    pfinal -->|YES| success
    pfinal -->|NO| err
```


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mark Loss Trap #1 — Install vs. Load:** Many students write `install.packages()` inside every script. This is **functionally correct** (idempotent) but loses 1–2 marks because examiners expect you to *state* that install is one-time and load is per-session.
> 2. **Mark Loss Trap #2 — Backslash vs. Forward Slash:** Writing `setwd("C:\lab")` will throw an error in R. Always use **forward slashes** or **double backslashes**.
> 3. **Mark Loss Trap #3 — Quoted Package Names:** `install.packages(dplyr)` will **fail** — package names *must* be quoted: `install.packages("dplyr")`.
> 4. **Mark Loss Trap #4 — Confusing `library()` and `require()`:** While both load a package, `library()` *throws an error* if the package is missing, whereas `require()` returns `FALSE` silently. Prefer `library()` in scripts.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between R and RStudio. Why is it necessary to install R before installing RStudio?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **R (1 mark):** R is a programming language and software environment for statistical computing and graphics. It is the *engine* that performs all computations and is developed and maintained by the R Development Core Team.
- **RStudio (1 mark):** RStudio is an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for R. It is a *graphical front-end* that provides an editor, console, environment pane, and plot viewer, but it does **not** contain the R interpreter itself.
- **Why install R first (1 mark):** RStudio is merely a *shell* that delegates all code execution to the underlying R interpreter. If R is not installed, RStudio will fail to launch with the error *"R is not found"* or *"Please install R before using RStudio"*. Therefore, R (the engine) must be installed first, and RStudio (the dashboard) is installed second.

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Question Paper]**
**Explain the difference between `install.packages()` and `library()` functions in R.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **`install.packages()` (1.5 marks):** Downloads the package from the CRAN repository and installs it **permanently** into the local R library folder. This is a **one-time** operation per machine per package. Example: `install.packages("ggplot2")`.
- **`library()` (1.5 marks):** Loads an already-installed package from the hard disk into the **current R session's** search path, making its functions directly accessible. This must be executed **at the start of every fresh R session**. Example: `library(ggplot2)`.
- The key difference is *persistence*: `install.packages()` writes to disk; `library()` only modifies the volatile session memory.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> Per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. You answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Past Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **four panes** of the RStudio IDE in detail, mentioning the function of each tab. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the step-by-step R procedure to **install the `dplyr` package from CRAN**, load it, use its `filter()` function on the `mtcars` dataset to display all rows where `cyl == 4`, and finally verify that the package is attached to the current session. Show all expected outputs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The RStudio IDE is divided into **four primary panes**, arranged in a 2x2 grid:

1. **Source Pane — Top-Left (1.5 marks)**
   - Acts as a text/code editor where you write, edit, and save R scripts (`.R`), R Markdown documents (`.Rmd`), and presentations.
   - Supports syntax highlighting, auto-completion, and `Ctrl+Enter` to send the current line to the console.
   - Multiple files can be opened in tabs.

2. **Console Pane — Bottom-Left (1.5 marks)**
   - The interactive R prompt (`>`) where commands are executed immediately.
   - Displays all output, warnings, and error messages.
   - The console is **non-persistent** — commands typed here are not saved to a file.

3. **Environment/History Pane — Top-Right (2 marks)**
   - **Environment tab:** Lists all variables, datasets, and user-defined functions currently in memory, along with their type (num, chr, logi, etc.) and dimensions.
   - **History tab:** A chronological log of every command executed in the session. Lines can be re-sent to the console with a double-click.

4. **Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer Pane — Bottom-Right (2 marks)**
   - **Files:** A graphical file browser of the current working directory.
   - **Plots:** Displays all generated graphs; can be exported as PNG, PDF, or SVG.
   - **Packages:** A clickable checklist of installed packages. A checkmark indicates the package is currently loaded.
   - **Help:** R's built-in documentation viewer; activated by `?function_name`.
   - **Viewer:** Renders interactive HTML output, such as Shiny apps and `plotly` widgets.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

```r
# Step 1: Install the dplyr package from CRAN (only first time)
install.packages("dplyr")

# Expected Console Output (abbreviated):
# trying URL 'https://cran.rstudio.com/bin/windows/contrib/4.4/dplyr_1.1.4.zip'
# Content type 'application/zip' length 1234567 bytes (1.2 MB)
# downloaded 1.2 MB
# 
# package 'dplyr' successfully unpacked and MD5 sums checked
# The downloaded binary packages are in
#     C:\Users\Student\AppData\Local\Temp\Rtmp...\downloaded_packages

# Step 2: Load dplyr into the current R session
library(dplyr)

# Step 3: Apply the filter() function to mtcars
result <- dplyr::filter(mtcars, cyl == 4)
print(result)

# Expected Output:
#               mpg cyl  disp  hp drat    wt  qsec vs am gear carb
# Datsun 710   22.8   4 108.0  93 3.85 2.320 18.61  1  1    4    1
# Merc 240D    24.4   4 146.7  62 3.69 3.190 20.00  1  0    4    2
# Merc 230     22.8   4 140.8  95 3.92 3.150 22.90  1  0    4    2
# Fiat 128     32.4   4  78.7  66 4.08 2.200 19.47  1  1    4    1
# ... (11 rows total)

# Step 4: Verify the package is attached to the search path
search()

# Expected Output (relevant portion):
# [1] ".GlobalEnv"        "package:dplyr"     "package:MASS"
# [4] "tools:rstudio"     "package:stats"     "package:graphics"
# [7] "package:methods"   "Autoloads"         "package:base"
```

**Evaluation Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Correct `install.packages()` command: **1 mark**
- Correct `library()` command: **1 mark**
- Correct use of `filter()` with the proper syntax: **2 marks**
- Correct expected output: **1 mark**
- Correct use of `search()` to verify attachment: **2 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Past Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** What is a **CRAN package** in R? Explain the three-stage lifecycle of using a package with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an R script to: (i) check the current working directory and change it to `C:/KTU/Lab2`; (ii) list all installed packages whose name starts with the letter `"M"`; (iii) install the package `MASS`; (iv) load it; and (v) generate a summary of the `survey` dataset from MASS. Show the expected output for each step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **CRAN package** (1 mark) is a formally structured, standardized collection of R functions, datasets, sample code, help files, and vignettes, bundled together to extend R's base capabilities. CRAN (Comprehensive R Archive Network) is the central repository hosted by R-project.org, mirroring thousands of such packages worldwide.

The three-stage lifecycle of using any R package is:

1. **Stage 1 — Installation (One-Time)** (2 marks)
   - Command: `install.packages("MASS")`
   - The package binary is downloaded from the chosen CRAN mirror and stored permanently in the local library directory (e.g., `C:\Program Files\R\R-4.4.1\library`).
   - This step writes to the **hard disk** and survives across R sessions.

2. **Stage 2 — Loading (Per-Session)** (2 marks)
   - Command: `library(MASS)`
   - The package is copied from disk into the current R session's memory and attached to the **search path** via `attachNamespace()`.
   - This must be re-executed in every fresh R session because session memory is volatile.

3. **Stage 3 — Usage (Per-Call)** (2 marks)
   - Once loaded, all exported functions and datasets of `MASS` become accessible by their short names.
   - Example: `survey$Sex` (uses the `survey` dataset), or `MASS::eqscplot(...)` (uses a function directly via the `::` operator).
   - This is the active phase where the package's statistical capabilities are actually invoked.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

```r
# (i) Check and set working directory
getwd()
# [1] "C:/Users/Student/Documents"

setwd("C:/KTU/Lab2")
getwd()
# [1] "C:/KTU/Lab2"

# (ii) List all installed packages starting with "M"
all_pkgs <- installed.packages()[,1]
pkgs_M <- all_pkgs[grep("^M", all_pkgs)]
print(pkgs_M)
# Expected Output:
# [1] "MASS"   "Matrix" "mgcv"   "mime"   "mnormt" "munsell"

# (iii) Install MASS (only if not already installed)
install.packages("MASS")

# (iv) Load MASS into the current session
library(MASS)

# (v) Generate a summary of the survey dataset
data(survey, package = "MASS")
summary(survey)
```

**Expected Output of `summary(survey)` (abbreviated):**
```
      Sex         Wr.Hnd      NW.Hnd      W.Hnd       Fold         Pulse   
 F:118   Min.   :13.00   Min.   :12.0   Min.   :13.00   L:99   Min.   : 35  
 M:118   1st Qu.:17.50   1st Qu.:17.5   1st Qu.:17.50   R:89   1st Qu.: 65  
         Median :18.50   Median :18.5   Median :18.50          Median : 70  
         Mean   :18.67   Mean   :18.6   Mean   :18.67          Mean   : 74  
         3rd Qu.:19.80   3rd Qu.:19.8   3rd Qu.:19.80          3rd Qu.: 80  
         Max.   :23.20   Max.   :23.2   Max.   :23.20          Max.   :130  
         NA's   :1       NA's   :1      NA's   :1              NA's   :45
```

**Evaluation Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Correct working directory commands: **1.5 marks**
- Correct filtering of packages by name pattern using `grep`: **1.5 marks**
- Correct install + load: **1 mark**
- Correct loading and summarizing of `survey` dataset: **2 marks**
- Correct expected output: **1 mark**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU Lab Viva and Exam:**

- ✅ **R is the engine; RStudio is the dashboard.** R must be installed **before** RStudio.
- ✅ **Four panes of RStudio:** Source (top-left), Console (bottom-left), Environment/History (top-right), Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer (bottom-right).
- ✅ **`install.packages("name")`** — downloads and installs once, quotes mandatory.
- ✅ **`library(name)`** — loads into current session, no quotes needed.
- ✅ **`require(name)`** — same as `library()` but returns `FALSE` on failure instead of erroring.
- ✅ **`pkg::function()`** — use a function from a package without attaching the whole package.
- ✅ **`search()`** — lists all attached packages in current session, in search order.
- ✅ **`installed.packages()[,1]`** — returns names of all installed packages.
- ✅ **`getwd()` and `setwd()`** — for working directory; always use **forward slashes** in paths.
- ✅ **`data()`** — loads built-in R datasets; `data(mtcars)` makes it accessible.
- ✅ **CRAN** = Comprehensive R Archive Network; default install source.
- ✅ **Help system:** `?function_name` or `help(function_name)` opens the documentation.
- ✅ **Default library folder** on Windows: `C:\Program Files\R\R-x.y.z\library`.
- ✅ **Command shortcuts:** `Ctrl+Enter` (run line), `Ctrl+L` (clear console), `Ctrl+S` (save script), `Ctrl+Shift+C` (comment block).
- ✅ **`.R` file** = R script; **`.RData`** = saved workspace; **`.Rhistory`** = command history.
- ✅ **`q()`** quits R and prompts to save workspace; **always answer `No`** unless you want `.RData` saved to the working directory.
- ✅ The R prompt **`>`** indicates R is ready; **`+`** indicates a continuation (an unclosed parenthesis or quote); **`?`** indicates R is waiting for a help-search keyword.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Familiarization of R Environment and R Studio, Installing and Using Packages.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic R Input/Output Commands and Simple R Programs Using Variables and Mathematical Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCBL308 - STATISTICAL METHODS LAB (R LANGUAGE) > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Basic R Input/Output Commands and Simple R Programs Using Variables and Mathematical Operations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the R programming language, **Input/Output (I/O) commands** constitute the foundational mechanism through which data flows between the user, the program's working memory, and the output console. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCCBL308**, basic R I/O commands comprise three primary operations: - **Input commands**: `scan()`, `readline()`, `read.csv()`, `read.table()`, `file.choose()` — used to accept data from the keyboard, files, or external sources. - **Output commands**: `print()`, `cat()`, `paste()`, `sprintf()`, `writeLines()` — used to display results on the console. - **Variable assignment**: Operators such as `<-`, `=`, and `assign()` for binding values to named memory locations. **Mathematical operations** in R follow standard arithmetic conventions with operator precedence, supplemented by a rich library of built-in functions: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^` (or `**`), `%%` (modulus), `%/%` (integer division), along with transcendental functions: `log()`, `exp()`, `sqrt()`, `sin()`, `cos()`, `tan()`, and so on. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 - Experiment 2):** > *"Familiarization of basic R input/output commands and writing simple R programs using variables and mathematical operations. Use of arithmetic, logical, and relational operators."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine R as a **scientific calculator with a memory and a notebook**: - The **Variables** are like labelled boxes in your desk drawer. When you write `x <- 25`, you are sticking a label "x" on a box and placing the number **25** inside it. Later, you can open that box, see the value, and use it in calculations. - The **Input commands** are like asking a colleague (the user) to hand you values: "Please give me your age" — `readline()` waits for keyboard input and stores it in a variable. - The **Output commands** are like a printer that displays results: `print(x)` announces the result formally, while `cat()` is like writing a casual note on a whiteboard. - The **Mathematical operations** are the calculator's buttons: `+` adds, `*` multiplies, `^` raises to a power, and `%%` gives the remainder after division. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** Variables *store* data, Input commands *collect* data, Output commands *display* data, and Mathematical operations *transform* data — together they form the bedrock of every R program you will ever write for statistical analysis. ## 1.3 Standard R Constants and Defaults | Element | Default Value / Standard | Description | |---|---|---| | `pi` | **3.14159265358979** | Mathematical constant π | | `letters` | **26 lowercase letters** | Built-in character vector `a` to `z` | | `LETTERS` | **26 uppercase letters** | Built-in character vector `A` to `Z` | | `month.name` | **12 month names** | Built-in character vector | | `TRUE / FALSE` | **Logical constants** | Boolean values (also `T` / `F`) | | `NA` | **Not Available** | Represents missing data | | `NaN` | **Not a Number** | Result of undefined operations (e.g., 0/0) | | `Inf` / `-Inf` | **Infinity** | Result of division by zero | > [!TIP] > R is **case-sensitive** — `X` and `x` are completely different variables. This is a frequent source of bugs for beginners. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anatomy of an R Statement Every R command line consists of: 1. **An expression** (the operation to perform) 2. **An assignment operator** (optional — used to store the result) 3. **A target variable** (the storage location) The general syntactic template is: ```r variable_name <- expression ``` If the assignment is omitted, R simply **evaluates** the expression and prints the result (auto-printing at the interactive prompt). ## 2.2 Variable Assignment — Three Methods | Method | Syntax | Example | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Leftward arrow | `x <- value` | `x <- 10` | **Preferred** R idiom | | Equals sign | `x = value` | `x = 10` | Allowed; common inside function calls | | `assign()` function | `assign("x", value)` | `assign("x", 10)` | Useful for programmatic name creation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The `<-` assignment operator is the **canonical R style** and is what the KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual and valuation key explicitly prefer. Avoid using `=` for top-level assignments in your record book. ## 2.3 R Input Commands — Exhaustive Reference ### 2.3.1 `scan()` — The Versatile Numeric Reader `scan()` reads multiple values from the console (or a file) into a vector. It is the most commonly tested input function in KTU lab exams. ```r x <- scan() # Reads numeric values from keyboard until a blank line ``` **Operational logic (step by step):** 1. R prints the prompt `1:` and waits for the first value. 2. Each entered value is appended to a numeric vector. 3. Typing an empty line (just press Enter) terminates input. 4. The completed vector is assigned to `x`. **Useful arguments:** | Argument | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `what = ""` | Allows character input | `scan(what = "")` | | `sep = ","` | Custom separator (CSV-like) | `scan(sep = ",")` | | `n = 5` | Read exactly 5 values | `scan(n = 5)` | | `file = "data.txt"` | Read from external file | `scan(file = "data.txt")` | ### 2.3.2 `readline()` — The Single-Value Reader `readline()` reads **one line of text** as a character string. To obtain a numeric value, wrap it with `as.numeric()`. ```r name <- readline(prompt = "Enter your name: ") marks <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter your marks: ")) ``` ### 2.3.3 File-Based Inputs | Function | Purpose | |---|---| | `read.csv("file.csv")` | Reads a comma-separated values file into a data frame | | `read.table("file.txt")` | Reads a whitespace/tab-delimited file | | `file.choose()` | Opens a graphical file-picker dialog | ## 2.4 R Output Commands — Exhaustive Reference ### 2.4.1 `print()` — The Formal Display ```r print(x) # Displays x with its [1] index marker print(x, digits = 4) # Controls displayed precision ``` The `[1]` prefix indicates the **first element's index** of the vector — a hallmark of R's vectorized nature. ### 2.4.2 `cat()` — The Flexible Concatenator `cat()` concatenates and prints without quotes, without the `[1]` marker, and supports custom separators. ```r cat("The value of x is", x, "\n") cat("Sum =", s, "\n", "Average =", avg, "\n") ``` **Key `cat()` arguments:** | Argument | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `sep` | Separator between items | `cat("A", "B", sep = "-")` → `"A-B"` | | `end` | Suffix (default newline) | `cat("Hi", end = "")` | | `file` | Write to a file | `cat("data", file = "out.txt")` | ### 2.4.3 `paste()` and `paste0()` — String Builders These **return** a string rather than printing it directly (unless wrapped in `cat()` or `print()`). ```r paste("Hello", "World", sep = " ") # "Hello World" paste0("Year", 2024) # "Year2024" (no separator by default) ``` ### 2.4.4 `sprintf()` — C-Style Formatted Output `Sprintf()` enables precise numeric formatting using format specifiers borrowed from C. ```r sprintf("Pi = %.4f", pi) # "Pi = 3.1416" sprintf("%d / %d = %.2f", 10, 3, 10/3) # "10 / 3 = 3.33" ``` | Specifier | Meaning | Example Output | |---|---|---| | `%d` | Integer | `42` | | `%f` | Floating-point | `3.141593` | | `%.nf` | n-decimal float | `%.2f` → `3.14` | | `%s` | String | `"R Language"` | | `%e` | Scientific notation | `3.141593e+00` | ## 2.5 Mathematical Operations — The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **canonical reference** for all arithmetic operators and built-in math functions. Memorize it thoroughly — KTU lab viva questions are frequently drawn from this list. ### 2.5.1 Arithmetic Operators | Operator | Operation | Example | Result | |---|---|---|---| | `+` | Addition | `7 + 3` | `10` | | `-` | Subtraction | `7 - 3` | `4` | | `*` | Multiplication | `7 * 3` | `21` | | `/` | Division | `7 / 3` | `2.333333` | | `^` or `**` | Exponentiation | `2 ^ 10` | `1024` | | `%%` | Modulus (remainder) | `7 %% 3` | `1` | | `%/%` | Integer division | `7 %/% 3` | `2` | ### 2.5.2 Mathematical Functions | Function | Purpose | Example | Result | |---|---|---|---| | `sqrt(x)` | Square root | `sqrt(16)` | `4` | | `exp(x)` | Exponential $e^x$ | `exp(1)` | `2.718282` | | `log(x)` | Natural log $\ln(x)$ | `log(10)` | `2.302585` | | `log10(x)` | Base-10 logarithm | `log10(100)` | `2` | | `log2(x)` | Base-2 logarithm | `log2(8)` | `3` | | `abs(x)` | Absolute value | `abs(-5.7)` | `5.7` | | `round(x, n)` | Round to n decimals | `round(3.14159, 2)` | `3.14` | | `ceiling(x)` | Round up | `ceiling(3.2)` | `4` | | `floor(x)` | Round down | `floor(3.9)` | `3` | | `trunc(x)` | Truncate toward zero | `trunc(-3.9)` | `-3` | | `signif(x, n)` | Significant digits | `signif(3.14159, 3)` | `3.14` | | `factorial(x)` | $x!$ | `factorial(5)` | `120` | | `choose(n, k)` | Binomial $\binom{n}{k}$ | `choose(5, 2)` | `10` | | `sin(x)` / `cos(x)` / `tan(x)` | Trigonometric (radians) | `sin(pi/2)` | `1` | | `asin(x)` / `acos(x)` / `atan(x)` | Inverse trig | `asin(1)` | `1.570796` | ### 2.5.3 Operator Precedence (Highest to Lowest) | Level | Operators | Associativity | |---|---|---| | 1 | `^` `**` | Right-to-left | | 2 | `-` `+` (unary) | Right-to-left | | 3 | `:` (sequence) | Left-to-right | | 4 | `%any%` (special operators) | Left-to-right | | 5 | `*` `/` | Left-to-right | | 6 | `+` `-` (binary) | Left-to-right | | 7 | `<` `>` `<=` `>=` `==` `!=` | Left-to-right | | 8 | `!` | Right-to-left | | 9 | `&` `&&` | Left-to-right | | 10 | `|` `\|\|` | Left-to-right | | 11 | `~` | Left-to-right | | 12 | `->` `->>` | Right-to-left | | 13 | `<-` `<<-` | Right-to-left | | 14 | `=` | Right-to-left | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These operators power real-world applications like digital signal processing (`sin()`, `cos()` for waveform generation), quality control (`round()`, `signif()` for reporting), probability computations (`factorial()`, `choose()` for binomial distributions), and financial modeling (`exp()` for compound interest). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Arithmetic Computation **Problem:** Compute the value of the expression $$z = \frac{a^2 + b^2}{a + b}$$ for $a = 8$ and $b = 6$. ### Step-by-Step R Code and Execution ```r # Step 1: Assign values to variables a <- 8 b <- 6 # Step 2: Compute the numerator and denominator numerator <- a^2 + b^2 denominator <- a + b # Step 3: Perform the division z <- numerator / denominator # Step 4: Display the result cat("Numerator =", numerator, "\n") cat("Denominator =", denominator, "\n") cat("Result z =", z, "\n") ``` ### Output ``` Numerator = 100 Denominator = 14 Result z = 7.142857 ``` ### Mathematical Verification $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= a^2 + b^2 = 8^2 + 6^2 = 64 + 36 = 100 \\ \text{Denominator} &= a + b = 8 + 6 = 14 \\ z &= \frac{100}{14} = \frac{50}{7} \approx 7.142857 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Interactive Program Using `readline()` **Problem:** Write an R program that accepts a student's name and three subject marks, then computes the total, average, and a grade letter based on the average. ### Step-by-Step R Code ```r # Step 1: Accept input name <- readline(prompt = "Enter student name: ") m1 <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter mark 1: ")) m2 <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter mark 2: ")) m3 <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter mark 3: ")) # Step 2: Compute total and average total <- m1 + m2 + m3 avg <- total / 3 avg <- round(avg, 2) # Round to 2 decimal places # Step 3: Assign grade using relational & logical operators grade <- ifelse(avg >= 90, "A+", ifelse(avg >= 80, "A", ifelse(avg >= 70, "B", ifelse(avg >= 60, "C", ifelse(avg >= 50, "D", "F"))))) # Step 4: Display the formatted report cat("\n------- STUDENT REPORT -------\n") cat("Name :", name, "\n") cat("Total :", total, "\n") cat("Average:", avg, "\n") cat("Grade :", grade, "\n") cat("-------------------------------\n") ``` ### Output ``` Enter student name: Ananya Enter mark 1: 85 Enter mark 2: 92 Enter mark 3: 78 ------- STUDENT REPORT ------- Name : Ananya Total : 255 Average: 85 Grade : A ------------------------------- ``` ### Step-by-Step Mathematical Trace $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total} &= m_1 + m_2 + m_3 = 85 + 92 + 78 = 255 \\ \text{Average} &= \frac{\text{Total}}{3} = \frac{255}{3} = 85.00 \\ \text{Grade} &= 85 \ge 80 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{A} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Using `scan()` to Read Multiple Values **Problem:** Read 5 numbers from the keyboard using `scan()` and compute their sum and mean. ```r # Step 1: Read values x <- scan() # Step 2: Compute s <- sum(x) n <- length(x) avg <- mean(x) # Step 3: Output cat("Entered values:", x, "\n") cat("Sum =", s, "\n") cat("Count =", n, "\n") cat("Mean =", avg, "\n") ``` ### Input Session ``` 1: 10 2: 20 3: 30 4: 40 5: 50 6: # <-- Blank line terminates input ``` ### Output ``` Entered values: 10 20 30 40 50 Sum = 150 Count = 5 Mean = 30 ``` ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Compound Interest Calculation **Problem:** Compute compound interest using the formula $$A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{n \cdot t}$$ where $P = 100000$ (principal), $r = 0.085$ (annual rate), $n = 4$ (compounded quarterly), $t = 5$ (years). Find the amount $A$ and the interest $I = A - P$. ```r P <- 100000 r <- 0.085 n <- 4 t <- 5 A <- P * (1 + r/n)^(n*t) I <- A - P cat("Principal =", sprintf("%.2f", P), "\n") cat("Annual rate =", sprintf("%.2f%%", r*100), "\n") cat("Compoundings/year =", n, "\n") cat("Years =", t, "\n") cat("Amount =", sprintf("%.2f", A), "\n") cat("Compound Interest =", sprintf("%.2f", I), "\n") ``` ### Mathematical Verification $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 100000 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.085}{4}\right)^{4 \times 5} \\ &= 100000 \times (1.02125)^{20} \\ &= 100000 \times 1.52530 \\ &= 152530.47 \\ I &= A - P = 152530.47 - 100000 = 52530.47 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Quadratic Equation Roots **Problem:** Given a quadratic equation $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$, compute its roots using the formula $$x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$ Test with $a = 1$, $b = -5$, $c = 6$ (expected roots: $x = 2$ and $x = 3$). ```r a <- 1 b <- -5 c <- 6 discriminant <- b^2 - 4*a*c cat("Discriminant =", discriminant, "\n") if (discriminant >= 0) { root1 <- (-b + sqrt(discriminant)) / (2*a) root2 <- (-b - sqrt(discriminant)) / (2*a) cat("Root 1 =", root1, "\n") cat("Root 2 =", root2, "\n") } else { cat("Complex roots: discriminant < 0\n") real_part <- -b / (2*a) imag_part <- sqrt(-discriminant) / (2*a) cat("Root 1 =", real_part, "+", imag_part, "i\n") cat("Root 2 =", real_part, "-", imag_part, "i\n") } ``` ### Mathematical Verification $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta &= (-5)^2 - 4(1)(6) = 25 - 24 = 1 \\ x_1 &= \frac{-(-5) + \sqrt{1}}{2(1)} = \frac{5 + 1}{2} = 3 \\ x_2 &= \frac{-(-5) - \sqrt{1}}{2(1)} = \frac{5 - 1}{2} = 2 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Student Pitfalls | # | Common Mistake | Correct Approach | Marks Lost If Wrong | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Using `=` at the top level instead of `<-` | Use `<-` for top-level assignment | Style deduction (-1) | | 2 | Forgetting `as.numeric()` around `readline()` | Always wrap: `as.numeric(readline(...))` | Wrong data type (-2) | | 3 | Using `/` instead of `%/%` for integer division | `%/%` for quotient, `%%` for remainder | Logic error (-3) | | 4 | Confusing `paste()` with `cat()` | `paste()` *returns*; `cat()` *prints* | Output error (-2) | | 5 | Missing `\n` newline in `cat()` | Always add `"\n"` at the end | Formatting deduction (-1) | | 6 | Using `^` for matrix power | `^` works element-wise; `%*%` for matrix multiplication | Wrong operation (-2) | | 7 | Typo in variable name (case sensitivity) | R is case-sensitive — `X` ≠ `x` | Runtime error (-2) | | 8 | Not terminating `scan()` with a blank line | Press Enter on an empty line to end input | Hangs the program | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — R Program Execution Flow The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete lifecycle of a simple R program that reads user input, performs mathematical computation, and displays the result. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Program Start] --> B[Assign Constants
e.g., pi 3.14159] B --> C[Display Prompt
readline or scan] C --> D{User Enters
Input Data} D -- Valid Data --> E[Store in Variable
x 25] D -- Invalid Entry --> F[Display Error
Warning Message] F --> C E --> G[Apply Mathematical
Operation e.g. sqrt exp log] G --> H[Assign Result to
New Variable y] H --> I[Format Output
print cat sprintf] I --> J[Display Result on
Console] J --> K[Program End] K --> L{Rerun
Required} L -- Yes --> C L -- No --> M[Terminate] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — R I/O Command Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart LR IO[R I/O System] IO --> INP[Input Commands] IO --> OUT[Output Commands] IO --> VAR[Variable Operations] INP --> INP1[scan] INP --> INP2[readline] INP --> INP3[read.csv] INP --> INP4[read.table] INP --> INP5[file.choose] OUT --> OUT1[print] OUT --> OUT2[cat] OUT --> OUT3[paste] OUT --> OUT4[sprintf] OUT --> OUT5[writeLines] VAR --> VAR1[Left Assignment
x less-than-dash value] VAR --> VAR2[Right Assignment
value dash-greater-than x] VAR --> VAR3[Equals Assignment
x equals value] VAR --> VAR4[assign Function
assign x value] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mathematical Operations Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD MATH[R Mathematical Operations] MATH --> ARITH[Arithmetic Operators] MATH --> REL[Relational Operators] MATH --> LOG[Logical Operators] MATH --> FUNC[Built-in Math Functions] ARITH --> A1[Add Plus] ARITH --> A2[Sub Minus] ARITH --> A3[Mul Star] ARITH --> A4[Div Slash] ARITH --> A5[Pow Caret] ARITH --> A6[Mod DoublePercent] ARITH --> A7[IntDiv DoubleSlash] REL --> R1[LessThan] REL --> R2[GreaterThan] REL --> R3[EqualsEquals] REL --> R4[NotEquals] REL --> R5[LessEq] REL --> R6[GreaterEq] LOG --> L1[And Ampersand] LOG --> L2[Or Pipe] LOG --> L3[Not Exclaim] FUNC --> F1[sqrt] FUNC --> F2[exp] FUNC --> F3[log log10 log2] FUNC --> F4[sin cos tan] FUNC --> F5[round floor ceiling] FUNC --> F6[abs factorial choose] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Operator Precedence Stack ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Top Priority Level 1] --> P0A[Caret and DoubleStar
Right to Left] P0A --> P1[Level 2 Unary Operators] P1 --> P1A[Unary Minus and Plus] P1A --> P2[Level 3 Sequence Colon] P2 --> P2A[a colon b creates sequence] P2A --> P3[Level 4 Special Operators] P3 --> P3A[percent any percent] P3A --> P4[Level 5 Multiply and Divide] P4 --> P4A[Star and Slash] P4A --> P5[Level 6 Add and Subtract] P5 --> P5A[Binary Plus and Minus] P5A --> P6[Level 7 Relational] P6 --> P6A[Less Greater Equal NotEqual] P6A --> P7[Level 8 Not] P7 --> P7A[Exclamation Mark] P7A --> P8[Level 9 And] P8 --> P8A[Ampersand and DoubleAmp] P8A --> P9[Level 10 Or] P9 --> P9A[Pipe and DoublePipe] P9A --> P10[Level 11 Tilde] P10 --> P10A[Tilde for formulas] P10A --> P11[Level 12 Right Assign] P11 --> P11A[MinusGreater and MinusGreaterGreater] P11A --> P12[Level 13 Left Assign] P12 --> P12A[LessMinus and LessLessMinus] P12A --> P13[Level 14 Bottom Priority Equals] ``` > [!NOTE] > The precedence pyramid above is the **single most important concept** for debugging expression-evaluation errors in R. Use parentheses `()` liberally to make precedence explicit — this is the KTU board examiner's preferred practice. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]** *Explain the difference between the assignment operators `<-` and `=` in R. Which one is preferred at the top level of an R script, and why?* ### Model Answer (3 Marks) - **(1 Mark)** The `<-` operator is the **canonical R assignment operator** that binds a value to a name in the enclosing environment, e.g., `x <- 10`. The `=` operator is a **syntactic shorthand** for assignment, primarily designed for use inside function calls (e.g., `mean(x = c(1, 2, 3))`). - **(1 Mark)** At the **top level of an R script**, `<-` is the **preferred operator** because it unambiguously indicates assignment, follows the official R style guide (Google's R Style Guide, Wickham's *R for Data Science*), and is the historical convention of the R community. - **(1 Mark)** Using `<-` at the top level avoids confusion between **assignment** (`x = 5`) and **argument passing** (`mean(x = 5)`) inside function calls, leading to cleaner and more readable code. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]** *Differentiate between `scan()` and `readline()` in R. State one practical scenario where each is most appropriate.* ### Model Answer (3 Marks) - **(1 Mark)** `scan()` reads **multiple values** (numeric or character) from the keyboard or a file into a **vector**. It continues accepting input until the user enters a blank line. `readline()` reads **exactly one line** of input as a **character string** and is typically wrapped with `as.numeric()` for numeric input. - **(1 Mark)** `scan()` is best suited for **entering a list of numbers** (e.g., a dataset of marks), such as: `marks <- scan()` to record 50 student marks quickly. - **(1 Mark)** `readline()` is best suited for **interactive prompts requiring a single value** (e.g., a username, password, or a single number), such as: `age <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter age: "))`. --- ## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks — Choice 1) ### **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Write an R program to read **two numbers** from the user and perform all **basic arithmetic operations** (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, modulus, integer division, and exponentiation). Display the results using the `cat()` function with appropriate labels. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the following with suitable R code examples: **(7 Marks)** - (i) The difference between `print()` and `cat()` - (ii) The use of `paste()` and `paste0()` - (iii) The use of `sprintf()` for formatted output ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) ```r # Step 1: Read two numbers from the user num1 <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter the first number : ")) num2 <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter the second number: ")) # Step 2: Perform all basic arithmetic operations add <- num1 + num2 sub <- num1 - num2 mul <- num1 * num2 div <- num1 / num2 mod <- num1 %% num2 intd <- num1 %/% num2 exp <- num1 ^ num2 # Step 3: Display results using cat() cat("\n------- ARITHMETIC RESULTS -------\n") cat("Addition :", add, "\n") cat("Subtraction :", sub, "\n") cat("Multiplication :", mul, "\n") cat("Division :", sprintf("%.4f", div), "\n") cat("Modulus :", mod, "\n") cat("Integer Division :", intd, "\n") cat("Exponentiation :", exp, "\n") cat("-----------------------------------\n") ``` **Sample Input/Output:** ``` Enter the first number : 17 Enter the second number: 5 ------- ARITHMETIC RESULTS ------- Addition : 22 Subtraction : 12 Multiplication : 85 Division : 3.4000 Modulus : 2 Integer Division : 3 Exponentiation : 1419857 ----------------------------------- ``` **Mark Distribution for Part (a):** | Component | Marks | |---|---| | Correct use of `readline()` with `as.numeric()` | 2 | | Correct implementation of all 7 operations | 3 | | Proper `cat()` formatting with labels | 2 | ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **(i) Difference between `print()` and `cat()` (2 Marks):** | Feature | `print()` | `cat()` | |---|---|---| | Output style | Adds `[1]` index marker for vectors | No index marker — cleaner output | | Quotes | Prints strings with double quotes | Prints strings **without** quotes | | Multiple args | Accepts only one main argument | Concatenates multiple arguments | | Newline | Automatic | Manual (must add `"\n"`) | **Example:** ```r x <- c(10, 20, 30) print(x) # [1] 10 20 30 cat(x, "\n") # 10 20 30 cat("Hello", "World", "\n") # Hello World print("Hello") # [1] "Hello" cat("Hello", "\n") # Hello ``` **(ii) Use of `paste()` and `paste0()` (2 Marks):** `paste()` concatenates strings with a **separator** (default is a single space). `paste0()` is identical but with **no separator** (equivalent to `paste(..., sep = "")`). ```r paste("R", "Language") # "R Language" paste("R", "Language", sep = "-") # "R-Language" paste0("Year", 2024) # "Year2024" paste0("File_", 1:3, ".csv") # "File_1.csv" "File_2.csv" "File_3.csv" ``` **(iii) Use of `sprintf()` (3 Marks):** `sprintf()` returns a formatted string using C-style format specifiers, ideal for precise numeric output. ```r sprintf("Pi = %.4f", pi) # "Pi = 3.1416" sprintf("%s scored %d out of %d", "Ananya", 85, 100) # "Ananya scored 85 out of 100" sprintf("Total = %.2f, Average = %.2f", 255, 85.00) # "Total = 255.00, Average = 85.00" ``` **Mark Distribution for Part (b):** | Component | Marks | |---|---| | `print()` vs `cat()` with examples | 2 | | `paste()` and `paste0()` with examples | 2 | | `sprintf()` with format specifiers and example | 3 | --- ## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks — Choice 2) ### **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Write an R program to compute the **area and circumference of a circle** given its radius (read using `readline()`). Use $\pi$ from R's built-in `pi` constant. Display the result rounded to 3 decimal places using `sprintf()`. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the following built-in R functions with syntax and one example each: **(7 Marks)** - (i) `sqrt()`, `exp()`, `log()`, `log10()` - (ii) `round()`, `ceiling()`, `floor()`, `trunc()` - (iii) `abs()`, `factorial()`, `choose()` ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **Formulas used:** $$ \text{Area} = \pi r^2, \qquad \text{Circumference} = 2 \pi r $$ **R Program:** ```r # Step 1: Read the radius r <- as.numeric(readline(prompt = "Enter the radius of the circle: ")) # Step 2: Compute area and circumference area <- pi * r^2 circumference <- 2 * pi * r # Step 3: Display results with 3 decimal places cat("\n------- CIRCLE CALCULATIONS -------\n") cat("Radius =", sprintf("%.3f", r), "\n") cat("Area =", sprintf("%.3f", area), "\n") cat("Circumference =", sprintf("%.3f", circumference), "\n") cat("-----------------------------------\n") ``` **Sample Input/Output:** ``` Enter the radius of the circle: 7 ------- CIRCLE CALCULATIONS ------- Radius = 7.000 Area = 153.938 Circumference = 43.982 ----------------------------------- ``` **Mathematical Verification:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= \pi \times 7^2 = 3.14159265 \times 49 = 153.93804 \\ \text{Circumference} &= 2 \times \pi \times 7 = 2 \times 3.14159265 \times 7 = 43.98230 \end{aligned} $$ **Mark Distribution for Part (a):** | Component | Marks | |---|---| | Correct input using `readline()` and `as.numeric()` | 2 | | Correct formula implementation | 3 | | `sprintf()` formatting with 3 decimals | 2 | ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **(i) Logarithmic and exponential functions (2 Marks):** | Function | Syntax | Purpose | Example | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | `sqrt(x)` | `sqrt(x)` | Square root $\sqrt{x}$ | `sqrt(144)` | `12` | | `exp(x)` | `exp(x)` | Exponential $e^x$ | `exp(2)` | `7.389056` | | `log(x)` | `log(x)` | Natural log $\ln(x)$ | `log(10)` | `2.302585` | | `log10(x)` | `log10(x)` | Base-10 log $\log_{10}(x)$ | `log10(1000)` | `3` | **(ii) Rounding functions (2 Marks):** | Function | Purpose | Example | Result | |---|---|---|---| | `round(x, n)` | Round to n decimals | `round(3.14159, 2)` | `3.14` | | `ceiling(x)` | Smallest integer $\geq x$ | `ceiling(3.2)` | `4` | | `floor(x)` | Largest integer $\leq x$ | `floor(3.9)` | `3` | | `trunc(x)` | Truncate toward zero | `trunc(-3.9)` | `-3` | **Important distinction:** `ceiling(3.2) = 4` but `trunc(3.2) = 3`; `floor(-3.2) = -4` but `trunc(-3.2) = -3`. **(iii) Miscellaneous math functions (3 Marks):** | Function | Purpose | Example | Result | |---|---|---|---| | `abs(x)` | Absolute value $\lvert x \rvert$ | `abs(-7.5)` | `7.5` | | `factorial(x)` | Factorial $x!$ | `factorial(6)` | `720` | | `choose(n, k)` | Binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ | `choose(10, 3)` | `120` | **Mathematical verification of `choose(10, 3)`:** $$ \binom{10}{3} = \frac{10!}{3! \cdot 7!} = \frac{10 \times 9 \times 8}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = \frac{720}{6} = 120 $$ **Mark Distribution for Part (b):** | Component | Marks | |---|---| | (i) `sqrt`, `exp`, `log`, `log10` with examples | 2 | | (ii) `round`, `ceiling`, `floor`, `trunc` with examples | 2 | | (iii) `abs`, `factorial`, `choose` with examples | 3 | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `as.numeric()` around `readline()`** (loses **2 marks**): `readline()` always returns a *character string*. Without `as.numeric()`, arithmetic operations will throw an error or produce nonsensical results due to string concatenation. > 2. **Confusing `/` with `%/%`** (loses **2 marks**): Use `/` for real division, `%/%` for integer quotient, and `%%` for remainder. Many students write `7/3` when they mean `7%/%3`. > 3. **Missing `\n` in `cat()`** (loses **1 mark** for formatting): Without `"\n"`, output runs together on a single line. > 4. **Using `paste()` to *display* output** (loses **1–2 marks**): `paste()` *returns* a string. To display it, wrap in `cat()` or `print()`. The KTU valuation key often deducts marks for this common slip. > 5. **Not rounding floating-point output** (loses **1 mark**): Raw `print(22/7)` gives `3.142857` — use `sprintf("%.4f", 22/7)` or `round(22/7, 4)` for clean output in lab records. > 6. **Case-sensitivity errors** (loses **1–2 marks**): Typing `Pi` instead of `pi` produces "object not found" error — R is strictly case-sensitive. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation:** - ✅ R is **case-sensitive** — `X` ≠ `x`, `Pi` ≠ `pi` (only `pi` is valid). - ✅ Use `<-` for top-level assignment; `=` inside function arguments. - ✅ `readline()` returns a **character** — always wrap with `as.numeric()` for numbers. - ✅ `scan()` reads multiple values into a vector; terminate with a **blank line**. - ✅ `print()` adds `[1]` index and quotes; `cat()` does not — use `cat()` for clean output. - ✅ `paste()` concatenates with a separator (default space); `paste0()` uses no separator. - ✅ `sprintf()` enables **C-style** format specifiers: `%d`, `%.nf`, `%s`, `%e`. - ✅ Arithmetic operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`, `**`, `%%`, `%/%`. - ✅ Operator precedence: `^` > `*/` > `+-` > relational > `!` > `&` > `|` > `<-` > `=`. Use parentheses liberally. - ✅ Built-in constants: `pi`, `letters`, `LETTERS`, `month.name`, `TRUE`, `FALSE`, `NA`, `NaN`, `Inf`. - ✅ Always include `"\n"` at the end of `cat()` calls for proper line breaks. - ✅ Rounding: `round(x, n)` (banker's rounding), `ceiling()` (up), `floor()` (down), `trunc()` (toward zero). - ✅ Special math: `factorial(x)` computes $x!$, `choose(n, k)` computes $\binom{n}{k}$. - ✅ Logarithms: `log(x)` is **natural** log $\ln(x)$; use `log10(x)` or `log2(x)` for other bases. - ✅ Trigonometric functions (`sin`, `cos`, `tan`) take arguments in **radians**, not degrees. - ✅ For KTU record books: every program should have a **commented header** (date, aim, algorithm) and a **handwritten output** section pasted below the code. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** KTU lab exams often test your ability to *combine* input + math + output into a single coherent program. Practice the patterns shown in Worked Examples 1–5 above, and you will be able to tackle **any** Module 2 experiment question with confidence.

topics.title: Control Statements in R (Switch, While, Repeat, Break, Next)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL308 - STATISTICAL METHODS LAB (R LANGUAGE)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control Statements in R (Switch, While, Repeat, Break, Next)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Control Statements in R?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Control statements in R are programming constructs that alter the sequential flow of execution of statements within a program. They govern the **decision-making**, **repetition (iteration)**, and **termination** behavior of a script. R provides three primary control structures: **selection statements** (`if`, `else`, `switch`), **loop statements** (`for`, `while`, `repeat`), and **flow-modifier statements** (`break`, `next`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCBL308 - Module 2):** Students are expected to write, trace, and debug R programs using `switch()`, `while()`, `repeat`, `break`, and `next` constructs. KTU lab examinations typically include a 1-hour R coding exercise where students must implement these structures on a dataset or numerical problem.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Railway Signal Box

Imagine a **railway signal box** controlling train movement on a junction:

| Control Statement | Railway Analogy | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **`switch()`** | A multi-track lever that sends a train to Track A, B, C, or D based on a single key | Multi-way branching from a single expression |
| **`while()`** | A gate that stays open as long as the signal is **green**; closes the moment it turns red | Entry-controlled loop (condition checked **before** entry) |
| **`repeat`** | A circular track that keeps running the train until the conductor manually pulls the **emergency brake** | Exit-controlled loop (no entry check; condition tested **inside**) |
| **`break`** | The **emergency brake** — instantly halts the loop | Terminates the innermost loop |
| **`next`** | The **"skip this station"** signal — passes the current iteration | Skips the remainder of the current iteration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** R is a **vectorized language**, so loops are often avoided in production code in favor of `apply()` family functions. However, for **KTU lab examinations**, you are **explicitly required** to demonstrate mastery over these traditional control statements.

### 1.2 The Five Control Constructs — A Quick Tour

**1. `switch(EXPR, ...)`**
A multi-way branch evaluator. The value of `EXPR` is matched against the names (or positions) of subsequent arguments, and the corresponding value is returned.

**2. `while(condition) { statements }`**
Evaluates `condition` first. If `TRUE`, executes the body; otherwise, exits. **Entry-controlled**.

**3. `repeat { statements }`**
Executes the body **infinitely** unless explicitly stopped. **No entry condition**; **no exit condition**. Only `break` can stop it.

**4. `break`**
Immediately terminates the **innermost** enclosing `for`, `while`, or `repeat` loop. Execution resumes at the statement after the loop.

**5. `next`**
Skips the rest of the current iteration and **proceeds to the next iteration** of the enclosing loop. (Equivalent to C's `continue`.)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** **"S-W-R-B-N"** → **S**witch, **W**hile, **R**epeat, **B**reak, **N**ext. Memorize this order; it is the order KTU question papers commonly use.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `switch()` Function — Multi-Way Selection

### 2.1.1 Operational Mechanism

The `switch()` function in R has two calling conventions:

**Form A — Position-based matching:**
```r
switch(EXPR, case1, case2, case3, ...)
```
Here, `EXPR` is coerced to an integer index (rounded down). The integer-th argument is returned.

**Form B — Name-based matching (most common in labs):**
```r
switch(EXPR, name1 = value1, name2 = value2, default = value)
```
Here, `EXPR` is matched (as a character string) against `name1`, `name2`, etc.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Evaluate the expression `EXPR`.
2. If `EXPR` is a **character string**, search for a matching argument name. If found, return its value.
3. If `EXPR` is a **numeric value** (and no name matches), coerce to integer `n` and return the `n`-th positional argument.
4. If **no match** is found, `switch()` returns `NULL` invisibly (Form A) or returns the value of the unmatched `default` argument (Form B).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `switch()` over nested `if-else`?** When there are **3 or more** mutually exclusive branches, `switch()` is **more readable**, **faster to type**, and **less error-prone** than deeply nested `if-else` chains. KTU examiners reward clean, idiomatic R code.

## 2.2 The `while()` Loop — Entry-Controlled Iteration

### 2.2.1 Operational Mechanism

A `while` loop guards the loop body with a **pre-test condition**. The condition is evaluated **before every iteration**, including the first. If `FALSE` initially, the body is **never executed**.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Evaluate the `condition` (must yield a single `TRUE` or `FALSE`).
2. If `TRUE`, execute the body; then go back to Step 1.
3. If `FALSE`, exit the loop; continue with the statement after the loop.
4. The variable involved in the `condition` **must be updated** inside the body, otherwise the loop becomes infinite.

> [!WARNING]
> **Infinite Loop Hazard:** Forgetting to update the loop counter in a `while` loop is the **#1 cause of R session freezes** in KTU lab evaluations. The examiner may award partial credit only if a `break` is present.

## 2.3 The `repeat` Loop — Exit-Controlled Iteration

### 2.3.1 Operational Mechanism

`repeat` is the most basic loop in R. It executes its body **indefinitely**. There is **no built-in exit condition**; you must insert a `break` statement (typically inside an `if` block) to terminate it.

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Execute the body unconditionally.
2. At some point inside the body, a condition is checked.
3. If the termination condition is met, `break` is triggered, and control transfers out of the loop.
4. If the condition is never met, the loop runs **forever** (this is the defining property).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use `repeat` at all?** It is **cleaner** than `while(TRUE)` and signals to the reader that the loop is intentionally infinite, with a deliberate break point. It is also **marginally faster** because R does not have to evaluate a condition before the first iteration.

## 2.4 The `break` Statement — Forced Termination

### 2.4.1 Operational Mechanism

`break` immediately halts the execution of the **innermost** enclosing loop. It does **not** terminate the program; execution resumes at the first statement after the loop. If used outside a loop, R raises an error: `"'break' is only allowed inside a loop"`.

### 2.4.2 Use Cases

- **Early exit** when a target value is found (e.g., searching an array).
- **Error handling** within `repeat` loops.
- **Stopping** a numerical algorithm when convergence is achieved.

## 2.5 The `next` Statement — Iteration Skipping

### 2.5.1 Operational Mechanism

`next` immediately abandons the **current iteration** and proceeds to the **next iteration** of the enclosing loop. Statements in the loop body that appear *after* `next` are **skipped** for that iteration.

### 2.5.2 Use Cases

- **Filtering** values (e.g., process only positive numbers).
- **Skipping invalid data** (e.g., `NA` values in a vector).
- **Avoiding division by zero** in iterative calculations.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax | Loop Type | Condition Check | Minimum Executions | Termination Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `switch()` | `switch(EXPR, ...)` | N/A (selection) | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| `while()` | `while(cond) { body }` | Entry-controlled | Before iteration | **0** (if cond is `FALSE`) | Condition becomes `FALSE` |
| `repeat` | `repeat { body }` | Exit-controlled | **Inside** body (via `if`) | **1** (always) | Explicit `break` |
| `break` | `break` | Flow modifier | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| `next` | `next` | Flow modifier | N/A | N/A | N/A |

| Comparison | `while` | `repeat` |
|---|---|---|
| Condition location | Top of loop | Inside loop (via `if`) |
| Risk of zero executions | **Yes** | **No** (always ≥ 1) |
| Infinite loop risk | If counter not updated | If `break` never triggered |
| Idiomatic R use | Known iteration count | Algorithm-based stopping |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Utility:** These control structures are foundational for **Monte Carlo simulations**, **iterative numerical methods** (Newton-Raphson, bisection), **data cleaning pipelines**, and **algorithmic problem-solving** — all of which are recurring themes in KTU lab viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: `switch()` — Position-Based Selection

**Problem:** Write an R program that accepts an integer (1–4) and prints the corresponding season name. Use `switch()` with positional arguments.

**R Code:**
```r
season <- function(n) {
  switch(n,
    "Spring",
    "Summer",
    "Autumn",
    "Winter"
  )
}

# Test cases
print(season(1))   # Spring
print(season(2))   # Summer
print(season(3))   # Autumn
print(season(4))   # Winter
print(season(5))   # NULL
```

**Trace Table:**

| Call | `n` | Coerced Index | Argument Returned | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `season(1)` | 1 | 1 | 1st argument | `"Spring"` |
| `season(2)` | 2 | 2 | 2nd argument | `"Summer"` |
| `season(5)` | 5 | 5 | None exist | `NULL` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** When the index exceeds the number of cases, `switch()` returns `NULL` — it does **not throw an error**. This silent failure can be a **common viva trap**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: `switch()` — Name-Based Selection

**Problem:** Implement a simple calculator using `switch()` with named arguments.

**R Code:**
```r
calculator <- function(a, b, op) {
  switch(op,
    "add"      = a + b,
    "subtract" = a - b,
    "multiply" = a * b,
    "divide"   = if (b != 0) a / b else "Division by zero!",
    "Invalid operation"
  )
}

# Test cases
print(calculator(10, 5, "add"))       # 15
print(calculator(10, 5, "subtract"))  # 5
print(calculator(10, 5, "multiply"))  # 50
print(calculator(10, 0, "divide"))    # "Division by zero!"
print(calculator(10, 5, "mod"))       # "Invalid operation"
```

**Trace Table:**

| Call | `op` | Match Found? | Returned Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| `calculator(10, 5, "add")` | `"add"` | ✅ | `10 + 5 = 15` |
| `calculator(10, 5, "subtract")` | `"subtract"` | ✅ | `10 - 5 = 5` |
| `calculator(10, 5, "mod")` | `"mod"` | ❌ | `"Invalid operation"` (the unmatched fallback) |

**Logic Walkthrough:**
1. The expression `op` is `"add"`. 
2. R searches for an argument **named** `"add"`. It finds `a + b`. 
3. It evaluates `a + b` and returns `15`. 
4. For `"mod"`, no name matches, so R returns the **unmatched fallback** `"Invalid operation"`.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: `while()` — Sum of First N Natural Numbers

**Problem:** Compute the sum $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} i$ using a `while` loop.

**Mathematical Foundation:**

$$S_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

We will verify our loop result against this closed-form formula.

**R Code:**
```r
sum_while <- function(n) {
  i <- 1
  total <- 0
  while (i <= n) {
    total <- total + i
    i <- i + 1
  }
  return(total)
}

# Test
n <- 10
result_loop <- sum_while(n)
result_formula <- n * (n + 1) / 2
print(result_loop)     # 55
print(result_formula)   # 55
print(result_loop == result_formula)  # TRUE
```

**Exhaustive Trace Table (n = 5):**

| Iteration | `i` (start) | `i <= 5`? | `total` (after) | `i` (after `i <- i+1`) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | TRUE | 0 + 1 = **1** | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | TRUE | 1 + 2 = **3** | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | TRUE | 3 + 3 = **6** | 4 |
| 4 | 4 | TRUE | 6 + 4 = **10** | 5 |
| 5 | 5 | TRUE | 10 + 5 = **15** | 6 |
| 6 | 6 | FALSE | — | — (loop exits) |

**Final result:** `total = 15`. Verification: $\frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15$. ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: `repeat` + `break` — Factorial Computation

**Problem:** Compute $n!$ using a `repeat` loop terminated by `break`.

**Mathematical Foundation:**

$$n! = \prod_{k=1}^{n} k, \quad 0! = 1$$

**R Code:**
```r
factorial_repeat <- function(n) {
  if (n < 0) return("Undefined for negative numbers")
  result <- 1
  k <- 1
  repeat {
    result <- result * k
    k <- k + 1
    if (k > n) break
  }
  return(result)
}

# Test
print(factorial_repeat(5))   # 120
print(factorial_repeat(0))   # 1
print(factorial_repeat(1))   # 1
```

**Exhaustive Trace Table (n = 4):**

| Iteration | `k` (start) | `result <- result * k` | `k <- k + 1` | `k > 4`? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 × 1 = **1** | 2 | FALSE | Continue |
| 2 | 2 | 1 × 2 = **2** | 3 | FALSE | Continue |
| 3 | 3 | 2 × 3 = **6** | 4 | FALSE | Continue |
| 4 | 4 | 6 × 4 = **24** | 5 | TRUE | **`break`** → exit loop |

**Final result:** `result = 24 = 4!`. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is `repeat` used here instead of `while`?** Because the loop body **must execute at least once** (we need to multiply by `k=1` even when `n=0`). The `repeat` construct **guarantees** at least one execution, which is its semantic advantage.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: `next` — Filtering Even Numbers

**Problem:** Print only the even numbers from 1 to 10 using `next` to skip odd numbers.

**R Code:**
```r
for (i in 1:10) {
  if (i %% 2 != 0) {
    next   # Skip odd numbers
  }
  print(i)
}
```

**Output Trace:**

| `i` | `i %% 2 != 0`? | Action | Printed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TRUE | `next` | ❌ |
| 2 | FALSE | Fall through | ✅ **2** |
| 3 | TRUE | `next` | ❌ |
| 4 | FALSE | Fall through | ✅ **4** |
| 5 | TRUE | `next` | ❌ |
| 6 | FALSE | Fall through | ✅ **6** |
| 7 | TRUE | `next` | ❌ |
| 8 | FALSE | Fall through | ✅ **8** |
| 9 | TRUE | `next` | ❌ |
| 10 | FALSE | Fall through | ✅ **10** |

**Output:** `2  4  6  8  10`

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative (vectorized R):** `print((1:10)[(1:10) %% 2 == 0])`. However, in KTU lab exams, the loop-based version is **expected** to demonstrate understanding of `next`.

---

## 3.6 Combined Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Construct | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Choose one of 5 menu options | `switch()` | Cleaner than nested `if-else` |
| Loop with a known count, condition at start | `while` | Pre-test prevents unnecessary work |
| Algorithm with internal stopping condition | `repeat` + `break` | Guarantees ≥ 1 execution |
| Search loop — exit when found | `for`/`while` + `break` | Early termination saves time |
| Process only valid data points | `for`/`while` + `next` | Filters out unwanted cases |
| Default case needed for unmatched inputs | `switch()` with fallback | Built-in unmatched-handler |

---

## 3.7 Numerical Verification Using the Closed-Form Formula

For the factorial example, we verify using Stirling's approximation (for large $n$):

$$\ln(n!) \approx n \ln n - n + \frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi n)$$

$$\text{For } n = 10: \quad \ln(10!) \approx 10 \ln 10 - 10 + \frac{1}{2}\ln(20\pi)$$

$$\ln(10!) = \ln(3628800) \approx 15.1044$$

$$\text{Approximation: } 10(2.3026) - 10 + 0.5 \ln(62.8319) = 23.026 - 10 + 2.0672 \approx 15.093$$

**Relative error:** $\frac{15.1044 - 15.093}{15.1044} \times 100\% \approx 0.075\%$. Stirling's approximation is extremely accurate, confirming our `repeat`-based computation yields $10! = 3628800$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing a Control Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Reaches Decision Point] --> B{Is it a Selection among multiple cases?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Number of cases > 2?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use switch with named arguments]
    C -- No --> E[Use if-else]
    B -- No --> F{Is it a Repetition task?}
    F -- No --> G[Sequential execution]
    F -- Yes --> H{Know iteration count upfront?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use for loop]
    H -- No --> J{Condition check needed at start?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Use while loop]
    J -- No --> L[Use repeat + break]
    L --> M{Is there a skip-filter requirement?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Add next statement]
    M -- No --> O[Add break for early exit]
    K --> M
    I --> M
```

## 4.2 `switch()` Execution Flow (Name-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: switch called with EXPR] --> B[Evaluate EXPR]
    B --> C{Is EXPR a character string?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Search for matching argument name]
    D --> E{Match found?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Evaluate and return matched value]
    E -- No --> G{Is there an unmatched fallback?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Return fallback value]
    G -- No --> I[Return NULL invisibly]
    C -- No --> J[Coerce EXPR to integer]
    J --> K[Return n-th positional argument]
```
