## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Series Analysis** is a statistical technique that analyses a sequence of data points collected, recorded, or observed at successive, equally spaced time intervals. The primary objective is to extract meaningful statistics and characteristics from the data to forecast future values based on historical patterns.

A **Time Series** is formally defined as an ordered set of observations

$$X = \{x_{t} : t \in T\}$$

where $x_{t}$ represents the value of the variable at time $t$, and $T$ is the index set representing discrete, equally spaced time points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average)** is a class of statistical models used for analysing and forecasting time series data. It is denoted as **ARIMA(p, d, q)** where:
> - **p** = order of the AutoRegressive (AR) part
> - **d** = degree of differencing (Integration) required to make the series stationary
> - **q** = order of the Moving Average (MA) part
>
> Proposed by **George Box and Gwilym Jenkins (1970)**, it remains the cornerstone of univariate time series forecasting.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a fisherman studying the **daily fish catch** at a coastal village over 10 years.

- You notice that **today's catch depends somewhat on yesterday's catch** (memory of the system) → this is the **AR (AutoRegressive)** component.
- You also notice that sudden storms a few days back affected the catch, and the impact slowly faded → this is the **MA (Moving Average)** component.
- However, the catch data has an **upward trend** (more boats, better equipment over years) → you must **difference** the data to remove this trend → this is the **I (Integrated)** component.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Intuition:** ARIMA is like teaching a machine to remember the past in two ways — *what happened before* (AR) and *what shocks hit the system before* (MA) — after first cleaning the data of any long-term trends (I). Once the machine has this memory, it can predict the future.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| AR order | $p$ | $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | Number of lagged observations used |
| Differencing degree | $d$ | $0, 1, 2$ | Order of differencing applied |
| MA order | $q$ | $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | Number of lagged forecast errors used |
| Confidence Level | $\alpha$ | **0.05** | Standard for hypothesis testing (stationarity) |
| Lag | $k$ | $\geq 1$ | Time distance between observations |
| Mean of Series | $\mu$ | — | Long-run average value |
| Variance of White Noise | $\sigma^{2}$ | $> 0$ | Variance of the error term $\varepsilon_{t}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Time Series

A time series $Y_{t}$ can be decomposed into four systematic components:

$$Y_{t} = T_{t} + S_{t} + C_{t} + I_{t}$$

where:
- $T_{t}$ → **Trend Component** (long-term progression, e.g., global temperature rise)
- $S_{t}$ → **Seasonal Component** (regular periodic fluctuations, e.g., ice-cream sales peak in summer)
- $C_{t}$ → **Cyclical Component** (non-periodic oscillations, e.g., business cycles)
- $I_{t}$ → **Irregular/Random Component** (residual noise, unpredictable shocks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Decompose?** Understanding each component lets us model the underlying process more accurately. In R, the `decompose()` and `stl()` functions perform classical and LOESS-based decomposition respectively.

### 2.2 The Three Building Blocks of ARIMA

#### A. AutoRegressive Model — AR(p)

The current value $X_{t}$ is a linear combination of its past $p$ values plus a random error:

$$X_{t} = c + \phi_{1} X_{t-1} + \phi_{2} X_{t-2} + \ldots + \phi_{p} X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_{t}$$

In compact Backshift operator form ($B X_{t} = X_{t-1}$):

$$\phi(B) X_{t} = c + \varepsilon_{t}$$

#### B. Moving Average Model — MA(q)

The current value $X_{t}$ is a linear combination of the current and past $q$ white-noise error terms:

$$X_{t} = \mu + \varepsilon_{t} + \theta_{1} \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_{2} \varepsilon_{t-2} + \ldots + \theta_{q} \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

In backshift form:

$$X_{t} = \mu + \theta(B) \varepsilon_{t}$$

#### C. ARMA(p, q) — Combined Model

$$\phi(B) X_{t} = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_{t}$$

#### D. ARIMA(p, d, q) — Differenced ARMA

After applying the **differencing operator** $\nabla = 1 - B$ a total of $d$ times:

$$\nabla^{d} X_{t} = (1-B)^{d} X_{t}$$

the series becomes stationary, and we apply ARMA to the differenced series.

### 2.3 The Box-Jenkins Methodology (Four-Step Process)

This is the **gold-standard procedure** for building ARIMA models and is a guaranteed high-yield topic in KTU exams.

1. **Identification** → Check stationarity (ADF Test, KPSS Test), plot ACF and PACF, decide $p, d, q$.
2. **Estimation** → Fit candidate ARIMA models using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE).
3. **Diagnostic Checking** → Analyse residuals via Ljung-Box Test, ensure no autocorrelation.
4. **Forecasting** → Generate point forecasts and confidence intervals.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AR(p) Process** | $X_{t} = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_{i} X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_{t}$ | Models memory from past values |
| **MA(q) Process** | $X_{t} = \mu + \sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_{j} \varepsilon_{t-j}$ | Models shock persistence |
| **ARMA(p,q)** | $\phi(B) X_{t} = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_{t}$ | Combined AR + MA |
| **ARIMA(p,d,q)** | $\phi(B) (1-B)^{d} X_{t} = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_{t}$ | Full non-stationary model |
| **Differencing** | $\nabla X_{t} = X_{t} - X_{t-1}$ | Removes trend |
| **ACF (Autocorrelation)** | $\rho_{k} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X_{t}, X_{t-k})}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X_{t}) \cdot \text{Var}(X_{t-k})}}$ | Measures linear dependence at lag $k$ |
| **PACF (Partial ACF)** | Correlation between $X_{t}$ and $X_{t-k}$ after removing effects of intermediate lags | Identifies AR order $p$ |
| **White Noise** | $\varepsilon_{t} \sim N(0, \sigma^{2})$ with $\text{E}[\varepsilon_{t}\varepsilon_{s}] = 0$ for $t \neq s$ | Innovation / error term |
| **Stationarity Condition (AR)** | Roots of $\phi(B) = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle** | Ensures stability |
| **Ljung-Box Statistic** | $Q = n(n+2) \sum_{k=1}^{h} \dfrac{\hat{\rho}_{k}^{2}}{n-k}$ | Tests residual autocorrelation |
| **ADF Test Hypothesis** | $H_{0}$ : Series is **non-stationary**; $H_{1}$ : Series is stationary | Stationarity test |
| **AIC Criterion** | $\text{AIC} = -2 \ln(L) + 2k$ | Model selection (lower is better) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

ARIMA models are extensively used in:
- **Economics & Finance** → Stock price prediction, GDP forecasting
- **Electrical Engineering** → Signal processing, load forecasting in power grids
- **Civil Engineering** → Rainfall and river flow forecasting
- **Mechanical Engineering** → Predictive maintenance using vibration sensor data
- **Inventory Management** → Demand forecasting in supply chains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In lab examinations, you will most often be asked to use the **`AirPassengers`**, **`nottem`**, or **`EuStockMarkets`** built-in R datasets to demonstrate ARIMA modelling. Memorise the sequence of R functions used.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From AR to ARIMA via Differencing

**Step 1 — Start with a non-stationary AR(1) process:**

$$X_{t} = \phi_{1} X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_{t}$$

This process is **non-stationary** when $\phi_{1} = 1$ (random walk).

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order differencing operator:**

$$\nabla X_{t} = X_{t} - X_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the original AR(1) equation:**

$$\nabla X_{t} = \phi_{1} X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_{t} - X_{t-1} = (\phi_{1} - 1) X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_{t}$$

**Step 4 — Define the new differenced series $Y_{t} = \nabla X_{t}$ and a new AR(1) parameter $\phi_{1}^{\ast} = \phi_{1} - 1$:**

$$Y_{t} = \phi_{1}^{\ast} Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_{t}$$

The differenced series is now stationary. This is the essence of the **I (Integrated)** component. For $d$-th order differencing:

$$\nabla^{d} X_{t} = (1-B)^{d} X_{t}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: ACF of an MA(1) Process

For an MA(1) process $X_{t} = \varepsilon_{t} + \theta_{1} \varepsilon_{t-1}$:

**Step 1 — Compute the variance at lag 0:**

$$\gamma_{0} = \text{E}[X_{t}^{2}] = \text{E}[(\varepsilon_{t} + \theta_{1} \varepsilon_{t-1})^{2}] = \sigma^{2} + \theta_{1}^{2} \sigma^{2} = \sigma^{2}(1 + \theta_{1}^{2})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the autocovariance at lag 1:**

$$\gamma_{1} = \text{E}[X_{t} X_{t-1}] = \text{E}[(\varepsilon_{t} + \theta_{1} \varepsilon_{t-1})(\varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_{1} \varepsilon_{t-2})] = \theta_{1} \sigma^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the autocovariance at lag $k \geq 2$:**

$$\gamma_{k} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compute the autocorrelation function (ACF):**

$$\rho_{1} = \dfrac{\gamma_{1}}{\gamma_{0}} = \dfrac{\theta_{1}}{1 + \theta_{1}^{2}}, \quad \rho_{k} = 0 \text{ for } k \geq 2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** An MA(q) process has an ACF that **cuts off sharply after lag q** and a PACF that **decays gradually**. Conversely, an AR(p) process has a PACF that cuts off after lag $p$ and an ACF that decays gradually. This duality is the foundation of order identification.

### 3.3 Exhaustive R Code Walkthrough — ARIMA on `AirPassengers`

Below is a complete, KTU-lab-ready R script with every step explained.

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 1: Load required libraries
# ============================================================
# 'forecast' provides auto.arima() and forecast() functions
# 'tseries' provides the adf.test() stationarity test
install.packages(c("forecast", "tseries"))
library(forecast)
library(tseries)

# ============================================================
# STEP 2: Load and inspect the built-in AirPassengers dataset
# ============================================================
# 'AirPassengers' is a classic monthly time series (1949-1960)
# It shows the trend + seasonality + multiplicative noise
data("AirPassengers")
print(class(AirPassengers))         # Should return "ts"
print(start(AirPassengers))        # 1949 1
print(end(AirPassengers))          # 1960 12
print(frequency(AirPassengers))    # 12 (monthly data)
print(summary(AirPassengers))

# ============================================================
# STEP 3: Visualise the original series
# ============================================================
plot(AirPassengers,
     main = "Monthly International Airline Passengers (1949-1960)",
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Passengers (in thousands)",
     col = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
# Observation: clear upward trend + increasing seasonal amplitude
# Conclusion: data is non-stationary and has multiplicative seasonality

# ============================================================
# STEP 4: Decompose the series into components
# ============================================================
decomp <- decompose(AirPassengers, type = "multiplicative")
plot(decomp)   # Plots Trend, Seasonal, Random, and Observed

# ============================================================
# STEP 5: Test for stationarity using Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
# ============================================================
# H0: Series is non-stationary.   H1: Series is stationary.
adf_test <- adf.test(AirPassengers)
print(adf_test)
# If p-value > 0.05, we FAIL to reject H0 => series is non-stationary
# (This is the expected outcome for AirPassengers)

# ============================================================
# STEP 6: Apply differencing to achieve stationarity
# ============================================================
# First differencing removes the trend
diff1 <- diff(AirPassengers, differences = 1)
plot(diff1, main = "After First Differencing",
     col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)
adf.test(diff1)   # Should now show p-value < 0.05 => stationary

# ============================================================
# STEP 7: Identify p and q using ACF and PACF
# ============================================================
acf(diff1, lag.max = 36, main = "ACF of Differenced Series")
pacf(diff1, lag.max = 36, main = "PACF of Differenced Series")
# Convention: 
#   - PACF cuts off at lag p  -> suggests AR(p)
#   - ACF cuts off at lag q   -> suggests MA(q)

# ============================================================
# STEP 8: Fit ARIMA model automatically
# ============================================================
# auto.arima() uses Hyndman-Khandakar algorithm + AICc minimisation
fit_auto <- auto.arima(AirPassengers)
print(fit_auto)
# Typical output for AirPassengers: ARIMA(2,1,1)(2,1,1)[12]
# The (2,1,1) part is non-seasonal; (2,1,1)[12] is seasonal.

# ============================================================
# STEP 9: Manually fit an ARIMA(p,d,q) model for learning
# ============================================================
fit_manual <- arima(AirPassengers, order = c(2, 1, 1))
print(fit_manual)

# ============================================================
# STEP 10: Diagnostic checking on residuals
# ============================================================
checkresiduals(fit_auto)
# Interpretation:
#   - Residuals should resemble white noise (mean ~ 0, constant variance)
#   - Ljung-Box test p-value > 0.05 means residuals are uncorrelated

# ============================================================
# STEP 11: Forecast future values
# ============================================================
future_forecast <- forecast(fit_auto, h = 24)   # Forecast next 24 months
plot(future_forecast,
     main = "ARIMA Forecast for AirPassengers (Next 2 Years)",
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Passengers (in thousands)")

# Display forecasted values numerically
print(future_forecast)

# ============================================================
# STEP 12: Measure forecast accuracy
# ============================================================
accuracy(future_forecast)
# Common metrics: ME, RMSE, MAE, MPE, MAPE, MASE, ACF1
```

### 3.4 Interpretation Matrix for ACF and PACF

| Process Type | ACF Pattern | PACF Pattern | Suitable Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AR(p)** | Decays gradually (tails off) | Cuts off sharply after lag $p$ | Use PACF to pick $p$ |
| **MA(q)** | Cuts off sharply after lag $q$ | Decays gradually (tails off) | Use ACF to pick $q$ |
| **ARMA(p,q)** | Decays gradually | Decays gradually | Try multiple $(p,q)$ pairs |
| **White Noise** | All within blue confidence bands | All within blue confidence bands | No model needed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing ACF and PACF roles. Remember — **PACF tells you $p$**, **ACF tells you $q$**.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ARIMA(p, d, q) Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Time Series X_t"] --> B["Apply Differencing d times"]
    B --> C["Stationary Series Y_t = nabla to the d X_t"]
    C --> D["AR Component order p uses past Y values"]
    C --> E["MA Component order q uses past errors"]
    D --> F["Combined ARMA Engine"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Residuals epsilon_t"]
    G --> H["Diagnostic Check"]
    H --> I{"Residuals are White Noise"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Forecast Future Values"]
    I -- No --> K["Re identify p d q and Refit"]
    K --> F
    J --> L["Output Forecasts with Confidence Intervals"]
```

### 4.2 Box-Jenkins Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Identification"] --> A1["Plot the series"]
    A1 --> A2["Apply ADF test for stationarity"]
    A2 --> A3{"Is series stationary"}
    A3 -- No --> A4["Apply differencing increment d"]
    A4 --> A3
    A3 -- Yes --> A5["Plot ACF and PACF"]
    A5 --> B["Step 2 Estimation"]
    B --> B1["Fit ARIMA p d q via MLE"]
    B1 --> C["Step 3 Diagnostic Checking"]
    C --> C1["Analyse residuals ACF"]
    C1 --> C2["Apply Ljung Box test"]
    C2 --> C3{"p value greater than 0.05"}
    C3 -- No --> A4
    C3 -- Yes --> D["Step 4 Forecasting"]
    D --> D1["Generate point forecasts"]
    D1 --> D2["Compute 95 percent confidence intervals"]
```

### 4.3 ARIMA Pipeline Summary (Linear View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Raw ts Object"] --> R2["plot ts"]
    R2 --> R3["decompose"]
    R3 --> R4["adf.test"]
    R4 --> R5["diff if needed"]
    R5 --> R6["acf and pacf"]
    R6 --> R7["auto.arima or arima"]
    R7 --> R8["checkresiduals"]
    R8 --> R9["forecast"]
    R9 --> R10["accuracy and plot"]
```

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define a Time Series. List its four main components with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Time Series** is a sequence of observations recorded at successive, equally spaced time intervals.

The four main components are:

1. **Trend ($T_{t}$)** → Long-term gradual increase or decrease. *Example: Global average temperature rising over decades.*
2. **Seasonal ($S_{t}$)** → Regular periodic fluctuations within a fixed period (e.g., a year). *Example: Electricity consumption peaking every summer.*
3. **Cyclical ($C_{t}$)** → Non-periodic long-term oscillations. *Example: Stock market boom-bust cycles.*
4. **Irregular/Residual ($I_{t}$)** → Unpredictable random shocks. *Example: Sudden drop in traffic due to a natural disaster.*

Formally: $Y_{t} = T_{t} + S_{t} + C_{t} + I_{t}$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Explain the meaning of ARIMA(p, d, q). What does each parameter signify?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **ARIMA(p, d, q)** stands for **AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average** model, used for forecasting non-stationary time series.

- **p (AutoRegressive order)** → Number of lagged observations of the series used in the model.
- **d (Integrated/degree of differencing)** → Number of times the series is differenced to achieve stationarity.
- **q (Moving Average order)** → Number of lagged forecast errors (white noise terms) used in the model.

The full form of the model is:

$$\phi(B)(1-B)^{d} X_{t} = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_{t}$$

where $B$ is the backshift operator, $\phi$ and $\theta$ are the AR and MA polynomial coefficients.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** What is stationarity? Explain the Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test in detail. How do you determine the order of differencing $d$ in an ARIMA model? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable R code, demonstrate how to build an ARIMA model on the `AirPassengers` dataset. Show all steps from data loading to forecasting. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Stationarity, ADF Test, and Order of Differencing (7 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Stationarity** means the statistical properties of a time series — mean, variance, and autocorrelation — remain constant over time. A series $X_{t}$ is **strictly stationary** if its joint distribution is invariant under time shifts, and **weakly stationary** if $\text{E}[X_{t}] = \mu$ (constant) and $\text{Cov}(X_{t}, X_{t+k}) = \gamma_{k}$ (depends only on lag $k$).

**Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) Test:**

The ADF test checks for a **unit root** in the series. The hypotheses are:

$$H_{0}: \text{Series is non-stationary (has a unit root, } \phi = 1\text{)}$$
$$H_{1}: \text{Series is stationary (no unit root)}$$

The test regression is:

$$\Delta X_{t} = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma X_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_{i} \Delta X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_{t}$$

The R command is `adf.test(series)`. **Decision rule:**

$$\text{If } p\text{-value} < 0.05 \Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_{0} \Rightarrow \text{Series is stationary}$$

**Determining $d$ in R:**

- Start with $d = 0$. Apply `adf.test()`.
- If non-stationary, apply `diff(series, differences = 1)` and retest.
- Continue incrementing $d$ until the series becomes stationary. Typically $d \in \{0, 1, 2\}$.

**Identification of $p$ and $q$:** Plot `acf()` and `pacf()` of the differenced series.

---

**(b) Complete R Code for ARIMA on `AirPassengers` (7 Marks):**

```r
# Install and load libraries
install.packages(c("forecast", "tseries"))
library(forecast)
library(tseries)

# Load the dataset
data("AirPassengers")
plot(AirPassengers, main = "AirPassengers Data", col = "blue")

# Check stationarity
print(adf.test(AirPassengers))   # Likely non-stationary

# First differencing
diff_series <- diff(AirPassengers, differences = 1)
plot(diff_series, main = "Differenced Series")
print(adf.test(diff_series))     # Should be stationary now

# Plot ACF and PACF
acf(diff_series, lag.max = 24)
pacf(diff_series, lag.max = 24)

# Fit ARIMA model automatically
model <- auto.arima(AirPassengers)
print(summary(model))

# Diagnostic check
checkresiduals(model)

# Forecast next 24 months
fc <- forecast(model, h = 24)
plot(fc, main = "Forecast for Next 24 Months")
print(fc)
```

**Sample Output Interpretation:** For `AirPassengers`, `auto.arima()` typically returns `ARIMA(2,1,1)(2,1,1)[12]`. The forecast plot will show the historical series in dark colour and the future predictions in blue with a shaded 80% and 95% confidence interval.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often forget to apply `library(forecast)` and `library(tseries)`. **Loses 1 mark.** Also, students sometimes mis-interpret ACF/PACF — remember PACF cuts off at $p$ for AR models.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the Box-Jenkins methodology for building an ARIMA model. List all four steps with their objectives. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the roles of ACF and PACF plots. Using a neat table, explain how ACF and PACF patterns help in identifying AR, MA, and ARMA processes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Box-Jenkins Methodology (7 Marks):**

The **Box-Jenkins methodology** is a systematic four-step procedure for identifying, fitting, and validating ARIMA models. It was proposed by **George Box and Gwilym Jenkins in 1970**.

**Step 1 — Identification (2 Marks):**
- Plot the time series to detect trend and seasonality.
- Apply stationarity tests like `adf.test()` or `kpss.test()`.
- Apply differencing (and seasonal differencing) until the series becomes stationary.
- Examine ACF and PACF plots to suggest candidate values of $p$ and $q$.

**Step 2 — Estimation (2 Marks):**
- Fit candidate ARIMA models (e.g., ARIMA(1,1,1), ARIMA(2,1,0), etc.) using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**.
- Compare models using information criteria: **AIC (Akaike Information Criterion)** and **BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion)**.
- The model with the lowest AIC/BIC is preferred.

**Step 3 — Diagnostic Checking (2 Marks):**
- Analyse residuals using `checkresiduals()`.
- Apply the **Ljung-Box Test**:

$$Q = n(n+2) \sum_{k=1}^{h} \dfrac{\hat{\rho}_{k}^{2}}{n-k} \sim \chi^{2}_{h-p-q}$$

- **Decision rule:** If $p$-value $> 0.05$, residuals are uncorrelated → model is adequate.

**Step 4 — Forecasting (1 Mark):**
- Use the validated model to generate point forecasts and confidence intervals using `forecast()` in R.

---

**(b) Roles of ACF and PACF + Identification Table (7 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACF (Autocorrelation Function)** measures the **total correlation** between $X_{t}$ and $X_{t-k}$ at lag $k$, including indirect effects.
>
> **PACF (Partial Autocorrelation Function)** measures the **direct correlation** between $X_{t}$ and $X_{t-k}$, after removing the effects of intermediate lags $1, 2, \ldots, k-1$.

**Identification Table:**

| Process | ACF Behaviour | PACF Behaviour | How to Choose $p$ and $q$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AR(p)** | Decays gradually (exponential or sinusoidal tail-off) | Cuts off sharply after lag $p$ | Choose $p$ = lag where PACF cuts off |
| **MA(q)** | Cuts off sharply after lag $q$ | Decays gradually | Choose $q$ = lag where ACF cuts off |
| **ARMA(p,q)** | Decays gradually (tails off) | Decays gradually (tails off) | Try several $(p,q)$ pairs and use AIC |
| **White Noise** | All lags within confidence bands | All lags within confidence bands | No modelling required |

**R commands used:**

```r
acf(series, lag.max = 30, main = "ACF Plot")
pacf(series, lag.max = 30, main = "PACF Plot")
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse "cuts off" with "decays". "Cuts off" means the value becomes **zero or statistically insignificant** after a certain lag, while "decays" means it gradually approaches zero over many lags.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ARIMA = AR + I + MA.** Three parameters $(p, d, q)$ fully describe the model.
- **Stationarity is mandatory** before fitting ARMA. Use ADF test and differencing to achieve it.
- **PACF cuts off at $p$** (for AR), **ACF cuts off at $q$** (for MA) — this duality is the heart of order identification.
- **Box-Jenkins** = Identification → Estimation → Diagnostic Checking → Forecasting.
- **AIC** (lower is better) is the standard for model selection in R.
- **Residuals of a good ARIMA model must behave like white noise** — use `checkresiduals()` and Ljung-Box test.
- **R functions you must memorise:**
  - `ts()` — create a time series object
  - `plot()` — visualise the series
  - `decompose()` / `stl()` — decomposition
  - `adf.test()` — stationarity test
  - `diff()` — differencing
  - `acf()` / `pacf()` — correlation plots
  - `arima()` — manual fitting
  - `auto.arima()` — automatic model selection
  - `forecast()` — generate forecasts
  - `checkresiduals()` — diagnostic check
  - `accuracy()` — error metrics
- **Common R datasets for KTU lab:** `AirPassengers`, `nottem`, `EuStockMarkets`, `lynx`, `LakeHuron`.
- **d is almost always 0, 1, or 2.** Higher values are rare and over-difference the series.
- **Confidence intervals widen** as you forecast further into the future — this is normal and not a bug.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
